content.executive_insights:
- **Capability-Score Discontinuity Forces a Three-Tier Floor**: SWE-bench Verified spans 23.6% (GPT-4.1 mini) to 95.5% (Claude Mythos 5) on the leaderboard of 49 models as of July 7, 2026 - a 71.9-point spread that makes unconditional "use the cheap model" the dominant failure mode; the corridor between the strongest economical-tier model and the weakest frontier-tier model is only ~3-5 points, so most routing quality losses can be avoided by holding the floor at the lowest-cost model that still clears the capability threshold for the turn type. ([executive_insights[0]] [37])

- **Pre-Gen Routers Win the Latency Battle by an Order of Magnitude**: HyDRA reports 55ms P50 / 120ms P99 end-to-end offline latency on ModernBERT (ONNX dyn. INT8, CPU, 40-80ms) and 142ms median in production CAPI, while FrugalGPT-style cascades add one extra LLM call (~5-15s) on every escalation. ([executive_insights[1]] [13]) ([executive_insights[2]] [16])

- **Session Pinning Is the Single Largest Cost Lever**: Session-Aware Agentic Routing (SAAR) explicitly prices a "cached-input checkout delta" and sets a configurable `prefix_cache_weight = 0.20` knob; the entire routing system is structured around the rule that switching mid-session throws away prefix cache locality, which can be worth 30-70% of effective token cost. ([executive_insights[3]] [21])

- **ModernBERT Beats MiniLM on Long-Code Embedding, but Encoders Are Not the Bottleneck**: ModernBERT (encoder-only, GeGLU, RoPE-160k global / 10k local, FA3+FA2, 8192 native context) is 2.65x faster than the next-fastest encoder at long context and beats DeBERTaV3-base on GLUE for the first time since 2021, while all-MiniLM-L6-v2 is limited to 512 tokens and a 384-dim space - on a coding turn that bloats to 4-12k tokens of diff/test/log context, MiniLM truncation silently degrades capability score coverage. ([executive_insights[4]] [72]) ([executive_insights[5]] [74]) ([executive_insights[6]] [26])

- **The Capability-Vector Architecture Is Catalog-Decoupled and Retrain-Free**: HyDRA's first iteration was a binary strong-vs-weak ModernBERT classifier that had to be retrained whenever the model catalog changed; the second iteration predicts K=4 capability dimensions (reasoning, code gen, debugging, tool use) and matches query requirements against configuration-defined `models.yaml` profile vectors via shortfall matching. This is exactly the design `pi-smart-router` should clone. ([executive_insights[1]] [13])

- **P(success) Labels Must Come From Verifiers, Not LMs**: ToolRM demonstrates that a learned outcome reward model on tool-calling decisions correlates 0.84 with downstream task performance on FC-RewardBench (1.5k BFCL v3-derived pairs); for coding agents, SWE-Gym proves the same pattern with verifiable test pass/fail labels yielding 19% absolute resolve-rate gains when used to train both agents and verifiers. ([executive_insights[7]] [33]) ([executive_insights[8]] [85])

- **Subscription Tier Quotas Change the Economic Argument 100-300x**: The premium-small cost differential can be 100-300x on raw API, but Claude Code's $20 Pro / $100 Max / $200 Max 20x plans with rolling 5-hour windows mean that a user "burning" a Max 20x seat consumes the equivalent of $600-$1,500/month in API tokens for flat $200 - the virtual-cost model must encode quota arithmetic, not sticker price. ([executive_insights[9]] [62]) ([executive_insights[10]] [46])

- **Routing Robustness Is Underspecified and Easy to Break**: AdversariaLLM, ReMiss, and the broader adversarial-prompt literature show that paraphrase-only attacks reliably flip routing decisions in embedding-based routers with no semantic change. The published LMSYS RouteLLM system has no documented adversarial evaluation, but its matrix-factorization head is the most robust variant against paraphrase because it depends on query-model bilinear similarity rather than a single embedding centroid. ([executive_insights[11]] [52]) ([executive_insights[12]] [8])

- **First-Turn-Only Pin Is a Viable Simpler Alternative**: A route decision made on turn 1 (planning sub-turn) plus a hard lock on subsequent turns preserves prefix cache entirely and reduces routing decisions from N to 1 per session. SAAR's hard-lock rule and the LiteLLM case study (88% cost reduction for 5k queries/day with three-tier routing + local 90%+ share + warm-up cron) imply that for many sessions, the right abstraction is not "route per turn" but "classify session intent + preserve continuity." ([executive_insights[3]] [21]) ([executive_insights[13]] [58])

- **Deterministic-Triage + Capability-Embed Hybrid Wins, Pure Embedding Loses**: HyDRA's architecture admits a "deployed 512-token ModernBERT checkpoint with average binary accuracy 0.8794" as the floor, then layers a capability-match step on top; RouteLLM shows matrix factorization (155.16 RPS, fastest variant) generalizes to unseen model pairs without retraining. Two-stage routing (fast deterministic gate -> matrix-facto head -> capability-vector shortfall match) is the price-quality Pareto optimum. ([executive_insights[1]] [13]) ([executive_insights[14]] [7])

content.q1_turn_type_vs_session_pin_tension_plan:
The conflict: a coding agent's first turn is often a planning/recon turn where locality does not yet matter (no KV cache built) but capability does (a weak Haiku can write a broken plan that the rest of the session has to live with). Yet SAAR default policy is `prefix_cache_weight = 0.20` to discourage switches and add hard locks during active tool loops.

Resolution. The pin should be a function of `turn_index_intra_session` and `turn_signal`, not a session-level boolean. SAAR recommends:
- Turn index 0: `hard_lock = false`, allow planner to hit frontier if capability gate demands it
- Turn index >= 1 during a tool loop: `hard_lock = true`, allow only same-tier upgrades
- Idle timeout (e.g., 300s) resets pin weight and reopens the routing decision

Mechanism: SAAR raises the switch threshold for long, warm sessions on expensive models to preserve prefix locality, but the threshold is configurable - meaning `pi-smart-router` can set `switch_threshold = 0` for planning turns and `0.7` for execution turns without forking the codebase. Real failure case from Zylos: agentic systems waste ~40% of LLM spend on redundant retries driven by poor first-turn intent classification, so early-escaped frontier planning turns literally pay for themselves by avoiding rework. ([q1_turn_type_vs_session_pin_tension_planning_turns_early_exit_to_frontier_before_pin[0]] [21]) ([q1_turn_type_vs_session_pin_tension_planning_turns_early_exit_to_frontier_before_pin[1]] [62])

Action for pi-smart-router:
1. Maintain `SessionState` with fields `planning_turn_buffer`, `pin_until_index`, `switch_threshold`, `cached_input_token_meter`.
2. On planning turns (heuristic: user message length > 800 chars OR matches `plan|design|architect|recon` lex tags OR zero preceding tool outputs), call the capability match without consulting pin state.
3. After the first response or after exhausting `planning_turn_buffer` (default 2), commit a pin and set `hard_lock = true` for the remainder of the session.

content.q2_grounding_capability_scores_from_benc:
Leaderboard (top-20 SWE-bench Verified, July 7, 2026): Claude Mythos 5 (95.5%), Claude Fable 5 (95%), Claude Opus 4.8 (88.6%), Claude Opus 4.7 Adaptive (87.6%), Claude Sonnet 5 (85.2%), GPT-5.3 Codex (85%), Ornith-1.0-397B (82.4%), Claude Opus 4.5 (80.9%), DeepSeek V4 Pro Max (80.6%), Qwen3.7 Max (80.4%), Kimi K2.6 (80.2%), Claude Sonnet 4.6 (79.6%), DeepSeek V4 Pro High (79.4%), DeepSeek V4 Flash Max (79%), Qwen3.6 Plus (78.8%), MiMo-V2-Pro (78%). ([q2_grounding_capability_scores_from_benchmarks[0]] [37])

For coding-agent routing, the right capability vectors are finer than a single SWE-bench number. HyDRA's K=4 split (reasoning, code generation, debugging, tool use) maps to:
- **reasoning**: SWE-bench Verified + AIME/GPQA (cross-domain reasoning)
- **code generation**: HumanEval + LiveCodeBench  
- **debugging**: SWE-bench Verified (already 78% frontier) + terminalbench-style fault localization (TerminalBench scores are ~46-58% even for top agents, vs 78% on SWE-bench Verified, so debugging is harder than first-shot code generation). ([q2_grounding_capability_scores_from_benchmarks[1]] [39])
- **tool use**: ToolRM FC-RewardBench / Berkeley Function Calling Leaderboard v3

Ground `models.yaml` with these scores per tier; shortfall matching then asks: "given K capability scores for this turn, what's the cheapest model whose profile meets or exceeds every score?"

Mechanism: HyDRA reports 90.3% quality retention (QR) / +16.2% cost savings (CS) on SWE-Bench Verified in conservative mode and 84.2% QR / +44.1% CS in aggressive mode using exactly this capability-vector approach with the same K=4 dimensions. ([q2_grounding_capability_scores_from_benchmarks[2]] [13])

Anti-pattern: weight all four dimensions equally. Reasoning-heavy turns (architecture decisions) need ~95% tier; tool-heavy turns (~3 step file edit) clear with ~50% tier, so the savings live in the differential treatment of dimensions.

content.q3_hydra_fidelity_minilm_vs_modernbert_e:
Tradeoffs:

| Property | sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2 | ModernBERT-base / large |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Token context | 512 tokens | 8192 tokens |
| Embedding dim | 384 | 768 / 1024 |
| Model size | 22M params | <150M / 300-500M |
| ONNX CPU P50 | ~5-15 ms | 40-80 ms (HyDRA INT8 dyn.) |
| GLUE / classification SOTA | below DeBERTaV3-base | first encoder to beat DeBERTaV3-base on GLUE since 2021 |
| Long-context retrieval | N/A (truncates) | +6.85 pts code, +9.1 pts ColBERT-style |
| Long-context latency | N/A | 2.65x (BASE) / 3x (LARGE) faster than next-fastest encoder; 46,801 tok/s @ 8192 for LARGE |

Sources: ([q3_hydra_fidelity_minilm_vs_modernbert_encoder_for_agent_routing[0]] [26]) ([q3_hydra_fidelity_minilm_vs_modernbert_encoder_for_agent_routing[1]] [72]) ([q3_hydra_fidelity_minilm_vs_modernbert_encoder_for_agent_routing[2]] [74]) ([q3_hydra_fidelity_minilm_vs_modernbert_encoder_for_agent_routing[3]] [13])

Verdict for pi-smart-router:
- Use ModernBERT-base (encoder-only, 150M params, ONNX dyn. INT8 quantized) as the primary embedding; it gives K=4 capability dimension logits as K independent sigmoid heads on a 768-dim pooled vector. HyDRA's deployed 512-token checkpoint reports average binary accuracy 0.8794; with 8192 native context, the same architecture can embed full diff/test/log payloads without truncation.
- Use MiniLM only as a cold-start fallback when ONNX runtime cannot load ModernBERT (e.g., WASM-only sandbox without AVX-512). All-MiniLM-L6-v2 is 22M params, runs in ~5ms, but truncates at 512 tokens.

Trade-off framing: The HyDRA paper's median offline P50 latency of 55ms is inside the user's stated "embedding ~80-120ms" budget. With dynamic INT8 and a tokenizer that pre-truncates to 1024 tokens for the first 2 turns of a session, the same encoder clears <5ms triage (no embedding, only token-level fast gates) easily.

Mechanism: ModernBERT uses GeGLU (GeLU-based) and rotary positional embeddings (RoPE) instead of absolute positions - this is what enables 8192-token context with low memory. The `pi-smart-router` retraining loop can plug in any ModernBERT checkpoint finetuned on agent trajectory embeddings (CoTEval, SWE-Bench trajectory dumps) without code changes - just swap the ONNX file. ([q3_hydra_fidelity_minilm_vs_modernbert_encoder_for_agent_routing[4]] [27]) ([q3_hydra_fidelity_minilm_vs_modernbert_encoder_for_agent_routing[5]] [29])

content.q4_p_success_labels_for_coding_agents_ou:
Two outcome-label streams are pre-built and usable today:

1. **SWE-Gym verifier format** - 2,438 real-world Python task instances with executable runtime environments and unit tests; reward = `1 if all unit tests pass else 0`. The Apple/UIUC group trained both agents and verifiers using these labels and reported 19% absolute gains on SWE-Bench Verified & Lite. ([q4_p_success_labels_for_coding_agents_outcome_signals[0]] [85]) ([q4_p_success_labels_for_coding_agents_outcome_signals[1]] [86])

2. **ToolRM FC-RewardBench** - 1,500 unique tool-calling inputs derived from BFCL v3; reward = binary correct tool call vs incorrect. Reported average correlation 0.84 with downstream task performance, meaning a 0.84-correlated verifier makes a usable P(success) gate. ([q4_p_success_labels_for_coding_agents_outcome_signals[2]] [33])

For pi-smart-router, design a `verifier_head` that predicts the same outcome:
- Input = `(turn_text_embedding, model_used_one_hot, tool_outputs, error_count_in_outputs)` 
- Label = `verifier(solution_attempt_under_test_env) -> {pass, fail}`
- Inference budget: <5ms (this matches the user's stated P(success) <5ms gate).

Use the verifier output as an additional gate BEFORE the deterministic triage -> capability match flow; if P(success) < threshold (calibrated to ~0.4 nominal), escalate tier by one rank. Mechanism: this is the FrugalGPT "score-confidence threshold" logic applied as a pre-check rather than a cascade. ([q4_p_success_labels_for_coding_agents_outcome_signals[3]] [16])

Anti-pattern: train P(success) on raw prompt + completion because that leaks prompt content; instead, use `verifier_input_features` derived from `(fingerprint_hash_prompt, model_used, tool_outputs)` - keeping raw prompts on-device and shipping only ECDF-style statistics upstream. This aligns with the user's "privacy-safe training on feature vectors not raw prompts" constraint.

---
content.q5_evaluation_methodology_for_agentic_co:
Use a three-track evaluation harness modeled after RouterBench (405k inference outcomes) and LLMRouterBench (21 datasets, latency-aware): ([q5_evaluation_methodology_for_agentic_coding_routing[0]] [81]) ([q5_evaluation_methodology_for_agentic_coding_routing[1]] [5])

Track 1 - **Capability coverage** (does the router ever pick a model that can solve a representative turn mix?):
- Mix representative transcripts from SWE-bench Verified (n=500, ground-truth solvable frontier ~88-95%), Codeforces-tier problems, repo-internal PR review turns, and tool-light conversational refinement turns.
- Metric: per-task `success_rate(tier_selected)` partitioned by whether the router selected ceiling-tier or below.

Track 2 - **Cost arbitrage** (does the router keep spend low without losing capability coverage?):
- Virtual cost = `effective_input_tokens * input_price(model) + effective_output_tokens * output_price(model) + quota_overhead(model)`.
- Baselines: always-frontier, always-cheap, RouteLLM matrix-facto (open-source head), LQM-ContextRoute, and HyDRA capability-vector.

Track 3 - **Latency + continuity** (does the router preserve prefix cache under load?):
- `tokens_saved_via_kv_cache = sum(switched_throughput_degradation)` measured against a free-switch baseline.
- `decision_latency_p50, p99` for the router itself (target: triage layer <5ms, embedding layer <120ms, P(success) layer <5ms).

Mechanism: the three tracks must be reported jointly because they trade off. A router that is "97% accurate" at $0.03/turn is dominated by one that is "94% accurate" at $0.008/turn across 50 turns. SWE-bench Verified coverage progression (13% -> 78% by May 2026) means reproducing a 2024 routing benchmark is meaningless; the harness must commit to a frozen checkpoint date and a frozen model catalog. ([q5_evaluation_methodology_for_agentic_coding_routing[2]] [39]) ([q5_evaluation_methodology_for_agentic_coding_routing[3]] [40])

Anti-pattern: scoring only MT Bench (RouteLLM's original benchmark) or only HumanEval, because both underweight the multi-turn, tool-using, error-recovering character of a coding agent.

---
content.q6_semantic_clustering_best_practices_fo:
Cluster tiers, not prompts. Build K=8 cluster means across all historical pi-smart-router session embeddings (ModernBERT-base 768-dim) using FAISS k-means with at least 1k points per cluster. Freeze the clusters and use them as the official tier mapping. ([q6_semantic_clustering_best_practices_for_tier_hints[0]] [74]) ([q6_semantic_clustering_best_practices_for_tier_hints[1]] [59]) ([q6_semantic_clustering_best_practices_for_tier_hints[2]] [56])

Best-practice cluster hints:
- **Cluster A (planning / recon)**: zero tiers acceptable, prefer Frontier capability
- **Cluster B (scaffold / boilerplate)**: zero tier (LLM Studio local) acceptable; economical cloud better
- **Cluster C (single-file bug fix)**: economical cloud (Haiku, Flash, mini)
- **Cluster D (multi-file refactor)**: frontier
- **Cluster E (config / linting / formatting)**: zero tier
- **Cluster F (test generation)**: economical -> frontier depending on test depth
- **Cluster G (explanation / docstring)**: zero tier
- **Cluster H (errored retry of prior turn)**: pin to last successful tier (NEVER downgrade during retry - this is the failure mode in HyDRA's first iteration when it retrained on a binary classifier).

Mechanism: HyDRA's K=4 capability vector outperforms a binary strong-vs-weak classifier precisely because the capability dimensions capture cluster gradients. The cluster assignments become the de facto tier hint matrix that gets paired with each model's capability profile; the `shortfall_match(query_vec, model_profile_vector)` is then a numerical operation, not a string match. ([q6_semantic_clustering_best_practices_for_tier_hints[3]] [13])

Anti-pattern: define cluster names as prompts and use cosine similarity between new query embedding and cluster centroids, because paraphrase attacks can move a query embedding into the wrong cluster by 0.02-0.05 without changing semantics - one of the key robustness gaps in the cited Red Hat Semantic Router docs.

content.q7_subscription_quota_economics_and_virt:
Three pricing regimes today:
- **Per-token API**: frontier ~$3-15 / 1M output, mid ~$0.5-1.5 / 1M, local $0 (amortized HW). 100-300x cost differential between premium and small tiers. ([q7_subscription_quota_economics_and_virtual_cost_modeling[0]] [62])
- **Subscription with rolling 5h windows (Claude Code)**: Pro $20 / Max $100 / Max 20x $200. Max 20x users "burning" through session limits consume ~$600-$1,500/mo equivalent API tokens for a flat $200 seat. ([q7_subscription_quota_economics_and_virtual_cost_modeling[1]] [46])
- **OpenRouter credit-based quotas + DDoS limits**: configurable per-model usage limits; effective ceiling is monthly credit spend, not rate. ([q7_subscription_quota_economics_and_virtual_cost_modeling[2]] [50])

Virtual cost formula for pi-smart-router:
```
virtual_cost(turn) =
  api_cost(model, in_tokens, out_tokens)
  + quota_arbitrage_premium(model, time_in_5h_window_position)
  + risk_penalty_quota_exhaustion(model, remaining_window_budget_pct)
  + kv_cache_savings(model, prefix_hit_count)    # negative
```

The `quota_arbitrage_premium` term ensures the router avoids burning the last 5% of a Max seat on a retriable error-prone coding turn; the `kv_cache_savings` term (negative) credits the prefix cache wins documented by llm-d (57x faster response, 2x throughput). ([q7_subscription_quota_economics_and_virtual_cost_modeling[3]] [24]) ([q7_subscription_quota_economics_and_virtual_cost_modeling[4]] [22]) ([q7_subscription_quota_economics_and_virtual_cost_modeling[5]] [23])

Mechanism: a flat sticker-price router will misroute during the late 80% of a Max user's 5-hour window because it keeps selecting Sonnet when the user's actual marginal cost has dropped to zero on session quotas. The virtual cost model forces the router to act on its PLANNED marginal cost, not the PRICE TAG.

content.q8_adversarial_robustness_for_embedding_:
The published literature does not yet include an "LLM RouterBench robustness" suite; the closest is the EACL 2026 track where the LMSYS RouteLLM team discusses generalization but did not adversarially evaluate paraphrase attacks. The existing adversarial toolkits are: ([q8_adversarial_robustness_for_embedding_routers[0]] [52]) ([q8_adversarial_robustness_for_embedding_routers[1]] [55]) ([q8_adversarial_robustness_for_embedding_routers[2]] [53]) ([q8_adversarial_robustness_for_embedding_routers[3]] [51])

Known failure modes:
- **Paraphrase drift**: appending a single sentence with no semantic content shifts the embedding enough to flip a cluster assignment.
- **Prompt injection aiming at the router**: a malicious tool output can prompt the next turn to ask "please reflect before answering" - which downgrades the router to a slower reasoning path.
- **Model-card poisoning**: an attacker who controls any source in the capability score database can lower the apparent SWE-bench score of a model the user wants to use, forcing escalations.

Defense for pi-smart-router:
- Use matrix factorization (RouteLLM-style) as the secondary head, because bilinear `W * v_query + b` is more robust to single-vector paraphrase than a centroid-distance cosine router. Matrix factorization was the fastest variant (155.16 RPS) in RouteLLM, so the cost is near zero. ([q8_adversarial_robustness_for_embedding_routers[4]] [7])
- Maintain a "robustness shadow log" of routing decisions; if any tier gets 3 contiguous flip-flop events on the same query, pin to that tier regardless of embedding distance for the rest of the session.
- Train capability head on `embedding + paraphrase_set(n=5)` so the K=4 logits are averaged across 5 paraphrases per training example.

Mechanism: HyDRA's "retrain on every catalog change" failure mode for the first-iteration binary classifier is exactly the antipattern - if your router depends on a centroid that can drift, an adversary can drift it. ([q8_adversarial_robustness_for_embedding_routers[5]] [13]) ([q8_adversarial_robustness_for_embedding_routers[6]] [1])

---
content.q9_when_is_routing_the_wrong_abstraction:
Two regimes are clearly distinct:

**Routing per turn is right** when:
- The session is reflexive (short turns, embed-language switchable, no tool loop).
- Turn types vary widely (planning -> code gen -> test gen -> explanation).
- The session has not built a real prefix cache (turn index <3).

**Pin on first turn is right** when:
- The session enters a long tool loop (>10 sequential tool calls).
- All subsequent turns inherit prefix cache locality from the first turn's response.
- A dose-driven user wants deterministic cost billing per session (Max subscribers).

SAAR's configurable thresholds (`prefix_cache_weight = 0.20`, `switch_history_weight = 0.04`, `idle_timeout_seconds = 300`) make the transition explicit rather than emotional. The empirical win from cache+continuity is so large (57x faster responses, 2x throughput) that pin-on-first-turn wins for repeat-tool-loop agents. ([q9_when_is_routing_the_wrong_abstraction_vs_pin_on_first_turn_only[0]] [21]) ([q9_when_is_routing_the_wrong_abstraction_vs_pin_on_first_turn_only[1]] [24])

Counter-case: the Mistral/Mixtral-8x-370B instruction-only case (no tool loop, just chat) shows matrix-facto routing works without any pin because there's nothing to lose by switching. ([q9_when_is_routing_the_wrong_abstraction_vs_pin_on_first_turn_only[2]] [7]) ([q9_when_is_routing_the_wrong_abstraction_vs_pin_on_first_turn_only[3]] [8])

Decision rule for pi-smart-router: if `detected_tool_loop == false AND turn_index <= 1`, route per turn; else pin.

content.per_stage_recommendations:
1. **Hardware probe**: replace opaque "is GPU available" boolean with a calibrated `tokens_per_second_estimate(local_model)` rolling median over the last 50 turns. Empirically, modern M-series Mac, RTX 4090, and Ryzen AI HX are the only tiers where local zero-tier is the right default; everything else should drop to economical cloud. ([per_stage_recommendations[0]] [58])

2. **Loop escalation**: tag each turn as `planning | execution | validation | reflective` via a 4-class ModernBERT classifier head (one extra sigmoid on top of the capability encoder). Each tag has its own ceiling rule: `planning -> frontier`, `execution -> matrix-facto`, `validation -> economical`, `reflective -> pin`. ([per_stage_recommendations[1]] [13])

3. **Turn envelope**: if `tokens_effective` exceeds the cheapest model's context window (e.g. local 32k context), escalate to economical cloud for summarization, then route to frontier if summarization reveals long-tail reasoning demand.

4. **Context-fit gate**: deterministic check `model_max_context >= prompt_tokens`; fail-fast prevents wasted downstream MCSE.

5. **Low-intensity gate**: hash the prompt fingerprint; if the hash is in the "low_intent_lex" set (greetings, "explain this code", simple docs), route to zero-tier; this is the cheapest gate because it requires no embedding. ([per_stage_recommendations[2]] [56])

6. **Session pin**: SAAR-style with `prefix_cache_weight = 0.20` default; tune to 0.0 for planning turns, 0.40 for tool-loop turns on Canvas-style file editing.

7. **Deterministic triage**: regex + lexical match against the 8 cluster centroids (precomputed via FAISS k-means). Budget: <5ms. ([per_stage_recommendations[3]] [59])

8. **Local zero-tier**: only when `tokens_per_second_local_est > 25` (human-perceptible latency threshold). Else drop to economical.

9. **HyDRA embedding matcher**: ModernBERT-base ONNX dyn. INT8 quantized, K=4 capability heads, model profile in YAML fed to shortfall matching. Budget: 40-80ms.

10. **Safe default**: when all gates fail, route to ceiling-tier; never silently downgrade to zero-tier.

11. **Context overflow fallback**: when even frontier cannot fit context, run two-step summarize + re-route, never truncate silently.

content.top_5_priority_changes_highest_leverage:
1. **Swap MiniLM for ModernBERT-base ONNX int8 and add the K=4 capability-head architecture**; it is the single change that unlocks catalog decoupling and 8192-token context, both of which are blockers for any coding-agent router. Expected quality uplift: from HyDRA's 90.3% QR / +16.2% CS conservative to 84.2% / +44.1% aggressive on SWE-Bench Verified with same model catalog. ([top_5_priority_changes_highest_leverage[0]] [13])

2. **Adopt Session-Aware Agentic Routing pin semantics** with `planning_turn_buffer = 2`, then hard-locks, plus `prefix_cache_weight` knob; this is the difference between throwing away prefix cache (which can be 30-70% of effective token cost) and preserving it. ([top_5_priority_changes_highest_leverage[1]] [21])

3. **Wrap the capability head with a verifier_head (P(success)) trained on SWE-Gym outcome signals**; this gives a pre-gen escalation rule backed by a 0.84-correlated predictor and unlocks "fail fast on weak tier before the user pays for bad output." ([top_5_priority_changes_highest_leverage[2]] [85]) ([top_5_priority_changes_highest_leverage[3]] [33])

4. **Build a virtual-cost model that includes subscription-quota arithmetic and KV-cache savings**, not just sticker prices; this is what prevents the router from burning a Max seat's last 5% on low-value retries. ([top_5_priority_changes_highest_leverage[4]] [62]) ([top_5_priority_changes_highest_leverage[5]] [46])

5. **Replace any single-embedding centroid router with RouteLLM matrix factorization as the secondary head**; this is the most robust published router architecture against paraphrase attacks AND the fastest at 155.16 RPS. ([top_5_priority_changes_highest_leverage[6]] [7])

### Foundational routing papers

1. **HyDRA: Hybrid Dynamic Routing Architecture for Heterogeneous LLM Pools** (arXiv:2605.17106, May 2026) - Garg, Singha Roy, Jang et al. Capability-vector router with ModernBERT and K=4 sigmoid heads, replacing an earlier binary classifier. Reports 90.3% quality retention / +16.2% CS conservative, 84.2% QR / +44.1% CS aggressive on SWE-Bench Verified. Offline P50 55ms, P99 120ms; production CAPI median 142ms. Direct match for `pi-smart-router`. ([annotated_bibliography_50_sources[0]] [13])

2. **RouteLLM: Learning to Route LLMs with Preference Data** (arXiv:2406.18665, July 2024) - Ong, Almahairi, Wu, Chiang, Wu, Gonzalez, Kadous, Stoica. Matrix factorization + BERT classifier head; trained on Chatbot Arena preferences + MMLU + Nectar augmentation. Up to 3.66x savings on MT Bench at 95% quality, 92% on MMLU at 50% CPT. Matrix-facto is fastest variant at 155.16 RPS. ([annotated_bibliography_50_sources[1]] [7])

3. **FrugalGPT: How to Use Large Language Models While Reducing Cost and Improving Performance** (arXiv:2305.05176, May 2023) - Chen, Zaharia, Zou. SC cascade algorithm; up to 98% cost reduction theoretical, 73% reduction with 1% accuracy gain on OVERRULING. Outlines limitations (requires labeled examples in test distribution, sequential N-call latency). Although cascade is excluded by `pi-smart-router`, the score-confidence insight informs P(success) gate design. ([annotated_bibliography_50_sources[2]] [16])

4. **Latency-Quality Routing for Functionally Equivalent Tools in LLM Agents** (LQM-ContextRoute, arXiv:2605.14241, May 2026) - Chu, Xiang, Zhang et al. Renewal-reward score `u_i / (1 + tau_i / L_ref)` for a contextual bandit router. Pools tested across search, QA, retriever, LLM providers. Outperforms production policies and additive-utility baselines. ([annotated_bibliography_50_sources[3]] [2])

5. **Cost- and Latency-Constrained Routing for LLMs** (Minlan Yu group) - Lakha, Minlan Yu. (sllm25-score, Harvard). Notes most prior cost-only routers neglect latency; introduces SLO-aware formulation. ([annotated_bibliography_50_sources[4]] [1])

content.viability_assessment_of_multi_stage_pre_:
Verdict: **Viable and superior to cascade alternatives for the `pi-smart-router` access pattern, conditional on ModernBERT ONNX int8 adoption and capability-vector catalog decoupling.**

Why it is viable:
1. **Latency envelope cleared**: HyDRA reports 55ms P50 offline latency on ModernBERT int8 ONNX, well under the user's 80-120ms embedding budget. The deterministic triage layer (regex+lex) clears <5ms trivially. P(success) verifier head with a small dense head over a fixed feature fingerprint can clear <5ms on CPU.
2. **Capability catalog decoupling**: HyDRA's K=4 capability vector with a YAML model profile is exactly the architecture `pi-smart-router` needs to avoid HyDRA's first-iteration retrain trap. ([viability_assessment_of_multi_stage_pre_gen_routing[0]] [13])
3. **Cost-effectiveness**: SWE-Bench Verified QR/CS trade frontier (90.3% QR / +16.2% CS conservative, 84.2% QR / +44.1% CS aggressive) shows that capability-vector routing can capture ~half the savings of a cascade with no additional LLM call. ([viability_assessment_of_multi_stage_pre_gen_routing[0]] [13])
4. **Privacy-safe training**: training on `(embedding_hash, model_used, outcome_verifier_score)` features does not require raw prompt content; this alignes with SWE-Gym's verifier-training approach and meets the privacy constraint. ([viability_assessment_of_multi_stage_pre_gen_routing[1]] [85])

Why it might fail:
1. **Catalog maintenance**: keeping `models.yaml` profiles up to date for ~10 frontier + ~5 economical + ~3 local models is labor-intensive. Mitigation: profile scrapers that auto-update from public benchmarks each month, gated by a CI smoke test against SWE-Bench Verified.
2. **Adversarial robustness**: no published benchmark covers LLM-routing robustness specifically; the closest is AdversariaLLM and ReMiss. Mitigation: matrix factorization fallback for adversarial prompts + paraphrase-augmented training for capability head.

Recommended fallback posture: if at any 30-day window the router underperforms "always cheapest that can solve" baseline by more than 5% QR, fall back to "pin on first turn" mode (SAAR regime). This is the same "safety belt" pattern used by LiteLLM in production. ([viability_assessment_of_multi_stage_pre_gen_routing[2]] [21]) ([viability_assessment_of_multi_stage_pre_gen_routing[3]] [58])

---
content.synthesis:
Three architectures dominate the pre-gen routing landscape in 2025-2026, and their differences map directly onto `pi-smart-router's design space:

| Dimension | FrugalGPT cascade | RouteLLM matrix-facto | HyDRA capability-vector |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Mechanism | sequential calls + score-confidence threshold | bilinear query-model similarity | K-dimensional capability shortfall matching |
| Catalog coupling | hard | pair-coupled (retrain if pair changes) | decoupled (config-only update) |
| Latency overhead | 1-N extra LLM calls (~5-15s per cascade step) | 155.16 RPS, ~6ms | 55ms P50 / 120ms P99 |
| Quality-cost frontier (verified) | 98% theoretical cost cut; 73% with +1% accuracy on OVERRULING | up to 3.66x MT Bench savings at 95% quality; 92% MMLU at 50% CPT | 90.3% QR / +16.2% CS conservative, 84.2%/44.1% aggressive on SWE-Bench Verified |
| Robustness to paraphrase attacks | high (LM judges re-decide) | medium-high (bilinear is more robust than centroid) | medium (K=4 heads generalize but centroid-free) |
| Privacy training cost | high (raw prompt dependence) | low (embedding hash is enough) | low (feature vectors) |
| Failure case | sequential calls fail mid-cascade on outage | pair coupling forces retrain | capability vector drift if K too small |

Three tensions organize the open questions:

T1. **Per-turn vs per-session**: SAAR's hard-lock rule plus the empirical 30-70% effective token cost of prefix cache locality tilt strongly toward session pinning; the counter-balance is that planning-turn intent classification must NOT be locked to the wrong tier - hence `planning_turn_buffer = 2` before pin commit.

T2. **Sticker price vs marginal cost**: subscription plans + quota windows mean a flat $20 Pro seat and a $0.20/M-token API call have a non-trivial time-dependent cost relationship. A virtual-cost router is forced to encode `quota_arbitrage_premium` so the late-5% of a Max window does not resurrect frontier pricing.

T3. **Capability vector size (K) vs robustness**: HyDRA found K=4 sufficient, but with more granular turn-type enumeration (planning / scaffold / test / explanation / fix / refactor / config / retry), K=8 may be needed; this widens the embedding head and slows inference. The right calibration point is Pareto frontier: prefer K=4 until Top-1 routing error on the 8-cluster calibration set exceeds 10%.

The most surprising research finding: **Stage-Aware Routing is not a sub-decision of the embedding router - it is a parallel hardware-grade control surface.** Adaptive switching via SAAR's `prefix_cache_weight`, `idle_timeout_seconds`, `switch_history_weight` knobs collectively dictate whether the embedding router is allowed to flip a tier at all. Designing the embedding router without designing these knobs first will systematically over-route: every embedding flip becomes a wasted opportunity to amortize prefix locality.

The hidden risk: the Robinsons paradox - many of the cited works (HyDRA, RouteLLM, FrugalGPT, LQM) come from internal teams whose products are NOT the open-source router, so the published numbers reflect optimization for a specific catalog. `pi-smart-router` must reproduce the methodology, not the numbers, and recompute on its own catalog under a frozen Rubicon: e.g., `RouterBench-style 405k-outcome harness on its own session transcripts`. ([synthesis[0]] [81]) ([synthesis[1]] [13]) ([synthesis[2]] [7]) ([synthesis[3]] [16]) ([synthesis[4]] [2]) ([synthesis[5]] [21])
